hints.text: Two plate types: **black w/ a black dot in the middle** (visible through blur) or white w/ **blue top bar**
hints.uniq_note: The black dot is Argentina-only; the blue bar also exists in Brazil.

shared_with.note: White plates w/ blue top bar exist there too.
Peru also has a black Gen3 car (and smallcam).
shared_with.diff: Chevrons: white-red → AR; landscape decides too.

shared_with.note: Uruguay also has a black Gen3 car.
shared_with.diff: Square poles → Uruguay; A-frame wood → Argentina.

hints.text: Very common: **round concrete poles w/ 3 alternating lines**; **doubled-up poles**; wooden poles w/ or w/o crossbar — **wooden A-frame poles = Argentina-only in S. America**
hints.uniq_note: A-frames seal it; doubled concrete poles also occur in Bolivia.

hints.text: Chevrons **white-and-red** — the only country in the Americas with these
Road lines: mostly **dashed white, double yellow, or a mix**
km markers white w/ **black top strip + road number**: **RN** = national (white label on the map), **RP** = provincial (green label)

shared_with.note: Same reflectors in Uruguay — but the guardrail SUPPORT shape differs.

hints.text: Default vibe: relatively **flat, treeless, sparsely populated** — wide-open desolate feel

bullets: Far west = mountainous (Andes); east rarely has mountains
Misiones: red soil (Brazil-like — check other clues!)
Chaco/Formosa: sandy soil + low trees & bushes
Pampas core: grassland w/ scattered tree plantations; small lakes → Buenos Aires province
San Luis: reddish pavement lined w/ needle-grass
Tierra del Fuego / SE-Santa Cruz: flat grass, almost no bushes; beech forests → TdF
Caranday WAX palm (low fan forests): Formosa (+ Chaco, Corrientes, N-Santa Fe)
Caranday palm (compact fans, dead-leaf coat): Sierras de Córdoba, San Luis
Butia yatay palm (arching, often leaning): Corrientes + El Palmar NP
Totai palm (thin leaves, spear top): wild in Formosa/Chaco/Misiones
Cardón cactus (candelabra, golden spines): high NW (Catamarca, Tucumán, Salta, Jujuy)
Pampas grass (white fluffy heads): Pampas region

hints.text: **Crops**: sugarcane → Tucumán (+ Salta/Jujuy); sorghum (dark-red heads) → Santa Fe/Córdoba/Buenos Aires; corn → Córdoba + Santiago del Estero; sunflower fields → S-Chaco (+ BA/S-Córdoba)

**Province infrastructure**: Latvian-style wooden **hook poles** → only La Rioja & Mendoza; single side insulator → Mendoza; white vertical pole stickers → Tucumán; unique provincial road markers → Córdoba; yellow T-junction signs w/ chevron pattern + road numbers → La Pampa

**Spotlights (north/west)**: Bolivia-like red bricks → W-Jujuy; salt pans → Jujuy (also SW-Bolivia/NE-Chile); red mountains → RN68 valley, S-Salta; Gen4 volcanic rocks → RP43 south of Antofagasta de la Sierra; RN65 (W-Tucumán) = bendy unpaved mountain road into the clouds; Talampaya NP (RN76, W-La Rioja) = red soil + barren ranges; lower mountains → west of Córdoba; **horse trekker** → SW-San Juan (similar one in Chile!); Mendoza outskirts = vineyards w/ Andes west

**Spotlights (south/east)**: Mar del Plata area = quartzite facades + orange tiles + vaulted porches; semi-coastal grass hills → NE of Bahía Blanca; monkey-puzzle trees → W-Neuquén (road 23; also Chile); **'sawdust' car** (sandy dust on black back) → centre-south; rock formations → Villa Dique Florentino Ameghino (NE-Chubut); Comodoro Rivadavia on Chenque hill slopes; snow coverage → eastern RP25 (E-Santa Cruz); melting snow → RN293 (SW-Santa Cruz); snowy mountains to the north → **Ushuaia** (southernmost city)
